name: Akshobhya Tirtha
dates_notes: Fourth pontiff of the Mādhva pīṭha-paramparā after Narahari-Tīrtha; preceptor of Jayatīrtha; sectarian chronicles plus manuscript colophons.

lineage_polemical.note: By Mādhva tradition Akṣobhya-Tīrtha defeats Vidyāraṇya/Mādhava in formal śāstrārtha at the Vijayanagara court on the meaning of tat tvam asi (Chāndogya 6.8.7), with Vedānta-Deśika as adjudicator; the verdict is recorded in the school's chronicles (and reflected in later Mādhva polemical literature culminating in Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta).

core_thesis:
Akṣobhya-Tīrtha is the fourth pontiff of the Mādhva *paramparā*, the preceptor of Jayatīrtha, and — by Mādhva tradition — the principal direct opponent of the Advaita establishment in the early Vijayanagara period. The doctrinal architecture is Madhva's, transmitted without revision: *pañca-bheda*; *svatantra-paratantra* with Viṣṇu as the only *svatantra*; the *bimba-pratibimba* relation between Viṣṇu and *jīva* with strict asymmetry; the realist reading of the *mahā-vākyas* against the Advaita identity-construal.

The contribution is in three registers. First, the *Tat-Tvam-Asi-Khaṇḍana* — Akṣobhya-Tīrtha's prose *prakaraṇa* on the Advaita reading of the *Chāndogya* 6.8.7 *mahā-vākya* (*tat tvam asi*). The work argues that the standard Advaita identity-reading (*aikya-śravaṇa*) is incompatible with the *Chāndogya*'s own surrounding context (*Chāndogya* 6.8–6.16, the *Śvetaketu-Uddālaka* dialogue read sequentially), and that the verse establishes *Viṣṇor sādṛśya-jñāna* — the *jīva*'s recognition of similarity-of-attribute to Viṣṇu within continued ontological dependence — rather than identity. The argument is the textual base for the *śāstrārtha* tradition records as Akṣobhya's victory over Vidyāraṇya/Mādhava at the Vijayanagara court (with Vedānta-Deśika as adjudicator); the verdict is recorded in the Mādhva chronicles and is reflected in later Mādhva polemical literature culminating in Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*. Second, the institutional: as preceptor of Jayatīrtha, Akṣobhya is the figure through whom the *paramparā* passes to the systematizer of the school's scholastic apparatus. Third, the cultic-institutional consolidation of the *Mūla-Rāma-vigraha* worship tradition received from Narahari-Tīrtha and transmitted forward to Jayatīrtha and the later *pīṭha*-succession. His significance is therefore the formal entry of the Mādhva school into the Vijayanagara public *śāstrārtha* arena (which Vyāsatīrtha will continue at higher scholastic pitch a generation later) and the institutional handover of the *paramparā* to its principal systematizer.

engaged_works.title: Tat-Tvam-Asi-Khandana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemical prakaraṇa preserved in the Mādhva manuscript canon and cited in later Mādhva polemical literature.

ascription_evidence.description: Cited in Mādhva chronicles in connection with the Vijayanagara śāstrārtha tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The *Tat-Tvam-Asi-Khaṇḍana* ("refutation of the [Advaita reading of] *tat tvam asi*") is Akṣobhya-Tīrtha's polemical *prakaraṇa* on the *Chāndogya* 6.8.7 *mahā-vākya*. The argument proceeds in three stages. (i) Contextual: the *mahā-vākya* is read within the *Chāndogya*'s own sequential frame (*Chāndogya* 6.8–6.16, the *Śvetaketu-Uddālaka* dialogue), where each occurrence of *tat tvam asi* (the verse is repeated nine times across 6.8.7–6.16.3) is preceded by a *dṛṣṭānta* — the *honey* analogy, the *rivers* analogy, the *banyan-seed* analogy, the *salt-in-water* analogy. The *dṛṣṭāntas* establish *sādṛśya* (similarity) and *avibhāga* (non-separation) within ontological dependence, not identity-from-eternity. (ii) Grammatical: *tat tvam asi* admits a *sāmānādhikaraṇya* construal (one referent, two co-referential terms — the Advaita reading) and a *sādṛśya-pratyāyaka* construal (two referents related by similarity-of-attribute — the Mādhva reading). Akṣobhya argues that the *sāmānādhikaraṇya* construal is grammatically possible but contextually excluded by the *dṛṣṭāntas*. (iii) Doctrinal: the verse establishes *Viṣṇor sādṛśya-jñāna* — the *jīva*'s recognition of similarity-of-attribute (*sat*, *cit*, *ānanda* in dependent form) to Viṣṇu within continued ontological dependence (*paratantrya*) — not identity, and not even *aupādhika*-identity. The work is the textual base for the *śāstrārtha* tradition records as Akṣobhya's victory over Vidyāraṇya at the Vijayanagara court (the verdict is famously commemorated in the Mādhva tradition by Vidyāraṇya's Sanskrit *muktaka* — *asminn aṣṭādaśe loke catur-daśa-nadī-pateḥ |* — accepting Akṣobhya's reading); it is the immediate ancestor of Vyāsatīrtha's later treatment of the *mahā-vākya*-question in the *Nyāyāmṛta*.
